title: Why Flat Chunks Miss Relationships
concept: Vector search finds similar text but misses explicit entity relationships spread across many documents.

exercises.prompt: To kick off our unit on GraphRAG, let's recap: What is the main goal of standard RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)?

options.text: Retrieving external documents to give an LLM factual context
Training a model from scratch on public web data
Translating English prompts into computer code

exercises.explain: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the foundational concept of giving LLMs access to external data sources to ground their answers. We are now exploring how to optimize this data structure.

cards.title: Connecting the Dots
cards.body: Standard vector search is great at finding needle-in-a-haystack facts. But what happens when you ask a question that requires connecting clues scattered across ten different haystacks?

cards.title: Flat Chunking
cards.body: Flat chunking breaks documents into independent blocks of text for vector search. Because these blocks are stored separately, the AI cannot see the 'big picture' connections that tie different chunks together.

cards.title: The Shredded Recipe Book
cards.body: Imagine a recipe book where each ingredient is on a separate shredded strip of paper. You can easily find all strips mentioning 'basil', but you cannot easily trace how basil interacts with olive oil and garlic to make pesto.

cards.title: The Missing Link Example
cards.body: If Document A says 'Alice works at Cyberdyne' and Document B says 'Cyberdyne built Skynet', a standard vector search for 'How is Alice connected to Skynet?' might return neither chunk because neither contains all those terms.

cards.title: The Embedding Illusion
cards.body: Many believe that simply increasing LLM context windows or using better embeddings solves relationship mapping. In reality, embeddings only measure similarity, not explicit logical connections or networks.

cards.title: Islands vs. Webs
cards.body: See how flat vector search views data as disjointed points, whereas a relational approach sees the web connecting them.

left.text: Isolated islands of text. The system knows they exist, but not how they connect.

right.text: Connected web. The system traces paths from one entity to another across files.

exercises.prompt: Why can't standard vector embeddings natively show that 'Company A' acquired 'Company B'?

options.text: They measure semantic similarity, not logical connections between entities.
They only work with single-word documents.
They force the LLM to ignore nouns completely.
They are too slow to run on modern computers.

exercises.explain: Vector embeddings measure how similar two pieces of text are in meaning, but they do not define or link specific entities (like people, places, or concepts) across your database.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Flat vector search treats text chunks as isolated units and does not map the web of connections between them.
exercises.explain: Vector search evaluates chunks independently. If a relationship is split across Document A and Document B, the vector search engine doesn't know they are connected.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the limitation of standard vector search.
exercises.sentence: Vector search finds similar text but misses the explicit relationships between entities in different chunks.
exercises.explain: Standard vector databases search for individual text segments that look similar to your query, but they fail to track the underlying relationships that span across different documents.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements about standard vector search is generated by a confused AI?

options.text: Standard vector search will retrieve chunks containing 'John' and 'TechCorp' separately, but might fail to synthesize the connection if they are far apart.
Standard vector search automatically creates a hidden database of family trees and corporate hierarchies to resolve this instantly.
Because the chunks are stored as isolated vectors, the system lacks an explicit link showing the relationship between John and TechCorp.

exercises.explain: The second answer is typical of an AI hallucinating or over-relying on basic keyword matching without recognizing that the database lacks the structured graph connections to link the entities accurately.

exercises.prompt: You are building a research assistant. Why does standard vector retrieval fail when you ask: 'How is Dr. Smith's research project connected to the funding cuts mentioned in the quarterly reports?'

options.text: It compares the exact character lengths of different files
It searches for semantic similarity but treats each text chunk as an isolated island
It automatically builds a web of connections before searching
It translates sentences into a graph structure by default

exercises.explain: Standard vector search looks for similar keywords and semantic meaning in isolated pockets of text. It cannot natively link 'Entity A' in Document 1 with 'Entity B' in Document 99 to reveal their relationship, as it treats all chunks as isolated islands.
